hero.title: MKS cookbook & reference
hero.description: Practical syntax, loops, entities, modules, stdlib, tests, and Python comparisons. Repo internals stay out of the way unless they affect code you write.

sections.title: Values & variables
sections.description: Bind with var, assign with =:, swap with <-->.
points: var name =: value; creates a variable in the current scope.
name =: value; updates an existing variable; array items can be updated with arr[index] =: value;.
The repo docs list numbers, strings, arrays, and null. The tests also cover booleans through true and false in std/bool.

sections.title: Expressions & operators
sections.description: Arithmetic, comparisons, and logical operators.
points: Arithmetic in tests: + - * / %.
Equality is spelled ?=; inequality is spelled !?.
Logical operators are && and ||; test cases print numeric truthy/falsy results.

sections.title: Control flow
sections.description: if/else, while, for, repeat, break/continue.
points: Every block starts with -> and closes with <-. Put statements inside the block and end normal statements with ;.
while repeats while its condition is true. Put the update inside the block or the loop may never finish.
for keeps init, condition, and step in the header: for (var i =: 0; i < n; i =: i + 1).
repeat N runs the body N times. repeat i in N also creates an iterator i with values from 0 to N - 1.
break; exits the nearest loop. continue; skips the rest of the current pass.
sections.source: Checked against repo docs and loop test cases: while_01.mks, for_01.mks, loop_control_01.mks.

tabs.label: MKS
steps: Use repeat 3 when you only need three passes.
Use repeat i in 3 when the body needs the pass number.
Use for when you need custom start, stop, or step logic.
Use while when the next pass depends on state changed inside the body.

sections.title: Functions
sections.description: Define, return, call, recurse.
points: A function starts with fnc name(args) -> and closes with <-.
Put return value; inside the body when the caller needs a value.
The repo tests cover normal calls, early return from if, and recursion.

sections.title: Swap & tests
sections.description: Fast in-place swap and built-in test blocks with expect.

sections.title: Entities
sections.description: Lightweight objects with init and method.
points: Entity parameters are passed into the constructor call: EntityName(a, b).
Use init to copy constructor parameters into self fields: self.name =: name;.
Methods are declared with method name(args) -> and can read or update self.
From the outside, read fields with dot access like obj.power and call methods like obj.boost(5).
sections.source: Checked against tests/cases/entity_01.mks and user-facing entity docs.

sections.title: Extend built-ins
sections.description: Attach methods to arrays and strings.
points: extend array adds methods to arrays.
Inside the method, self is the concrete array the method was called on.
To loop over self, use self.len() and indexed reads like self[i].
Return explicitly with return when the method should give a value back to the caller.
sections.source: Checked against tests/cases/extend_array_01.mks and user-facing extend docs.

sections.title: Watch / on change
sections.description: Reactive primitives built into the language surface.
points: watch x; and on change x must refer to the same name.
Register the handler before the change; otherwise that specific assignment has already happened.
The handler runs when the watched name is assigned.
Keep handlers short: print something, update a counter, or run a small reaction.

tabs.label: MKS
tabs.note: Register on change before the assignment you want to observe.

sections.title: defer
sections.description: Guaranteed cleanup on block exit.
points: Read defer as “do this when leaving the block”.
The defer body is written as a normal -> ... <- block.
When several defers are registered, current behavior runs them in reverse registration order.
Use this for cleanup-style demos, but do not invent file APIs that are not documented for MKS.

sections.title: Imports
sections.description: Simple modules with using. Imported top-level definitions become available in the caller environment.
points: using "std/math"; loads a standard module from the std path.
using "math.mks"; loads a local file and runs it once.
Definitions from the imported file become directly available in the current environment.
Alias-style access like m.add() is not used in examples because the public docs/tests show direct access to imported names.
sections.warning: Do not use using "x" as m; m.fn() as the main example. In the current user-facing docs/tests, the reliable pattern is using "file.mks"; plus direct calls to imported names.

tabs.label: MKS
tabs.note: Resolution order: current file directory -> current working directory -> installed std path. A module runs once; duplicate and nested imports are skipped.

sections.title: Stdlib: math, bool, array, string, time
sections.description: Bundled std/ files are plain MKS wrappers around native methods and functions.
points: std/math: abs, min, max, square, plus wrappers for floor, ceil, round, sqrt, pow, sin, cos, rand, randint.
std/bool: not, and, or.
std/array: helpers such as push, pop, first, last, slice, reverse, map, filter, reduce, sum, avg.
std/string: wrappers for len, lower, upper, trim, contains, starts_with, ends_with, replace, split, join.
std/time: wrappers for now, timestamp, and sleep(ms).

sections.title: Cookbook: sum numbers
sections.description: A practical loop shape for aggregating an array. Put the accumulator and index before the loop, then update both inside the loop.
points: var total =: 0; lives before the loop because the value must survive every loop pass.
var i =: 0; is also declared before the loop: it is the current array index.
Inside the while, first add nums[i] to the total, then always advance with i =: i + 1;.
If you forget to update the index, i < nums.len() will not change and the loop will not finish.
sections.source: Based on documented arrays, while, len(), and assignment behavior from repo tests/docs.

sections.title: Cookbook: safe last item
sections.description: Return null when the array is empty; otherwise return the last indexed value.
points: Check for an empty array first: an empty array has no last index to read.
return null; means “there is no value” here, not “the program crashed”.
For a non-empty array, the last index is arr.len() - 1.
This kind of helper is useful when an empty array is a normal case.

sections.title: Cookbook: watched counter
sections.description: Use watch when a named variable should announce changes. Register the handler before the writes you care about.
points: watch score; registers the variable name to watch.
on change score -> defines the code that runs when the value changes.
Declare the handler before the assignments you want to catch.
Inside the handler, read the current score and print it or run another small reaction.

sections.title: Standard library: std/math
sections.description: Import std/math for small numeric helpers. The repository includes pure MKS wrappers around native math functions.
points: Import std/math once near the top of a file that needs math helpers.
square(x) keeps code readable when you would otherwise repeat x * x.
min and max are cleaner than hand-written if blocks when you need a boundary value.
For helpers like sqrt and pow, keep examples simple: detailed edge cases are not fully documented yet.

sections.title: Standard library: std/array
sections.description: std/array gives function-style helpers over array methods.
points: std/array gives you a function-style API over array methods.
push(xs, value) mutates the array and returns that same array according to the stdlib source.
first(xs) and last(xs) are useful when you do not want to write indexes by hand.
sum(xs) and avg(xs) cover common numeric-array calculations.

sections.title: Standard library: std/string
sections.description: std/string exposes wrappers for string cleanup, casing, checks, replace, split, and join.
points: Import std/string when you want to call string operations as functions.
upper and lower are useful for normalizing user input.
contains(s, sub) answers “does this string contain that substring?”
For method-style calls, see the strings section: s.upper(), s.lower(), s.len().

sections.title: Standard library: std/bool
sections.description: std/bool provides function-style boolean helpers.
points: std/bool is useful when you want function-style boolean helpers.
not(x) inverts a truthy/falsy value.
and(a, b) and or(a, b) express normal boolean logic as functions.
For ordinary conditions, you can use && and || directly.

sections.title: Standard library: std/time
sections.description: std/time exposes current-time helpers and sleep(ms). Exact formatting is runtime-defined, so examples keep claims narrow.
points: now() and timestamp() come from time helpers.
sleep(ms) takes milliseconds according to the helper naming and examples.
Do not make user code depend on the exact now() output format until public docs lock it down.
For time demos, show the call itself and a minimal “wait, then continue” scenario.

sections.title: Cookbook: split code into a module
sections.description: Put reusable values/functions in one file, then load it with using. Imported names are shown as direct calls because that is the reliable pattern in current docs/tests.
sections.warning: Avoid using "x" as m; m.fn() examples for now. Alias is parsed but not used by current evaluation.
points: Move reusable variables and functions into a separate .mks file.
In the main file, load it with using "file.mks";.
After import, top-level names are available directly: circle_area(2), not m.circle_area(2).
Duplicate imports should be skipped according to README and test behavior.

sections.title: Cookbook: small test suite
sections.description: Use test and expect to keep examples executable as behavior checks.
points: First declare the function you want to test.
Then write test "name" -> to give the check a readable name.
expect(condition); should receive an expression that is expected to pass.
Keep this block near the example so the example is both a demo and a behavior check.

sections.title: Arrays & indexing
sections.description: Arrays are part of the core expression surface: create lists, read by index, assign by index, and extend array behavior with methods.
points: Create arrays with [1, 2, 3] or the empty literal [].
Read with an index: arr[0]. Tests use zero-based indexing.
Assign to an element with arr[1] =: 99;.
Tests use inject(value) to grow arrays and len() to read length.

tabs.label: MKS
tabs.note: The repository README lists arrays, indexing, and extend built-ins as core language features.

sections.title: Functions & recursion guard
sections.description: Functions support recursion. The interpreter README also calls out a recursion-depth guard, so runaway calls should fail with a clearer runtime error instead of silently eating the process.

sections.title: Comparisons & boolean flow
sections.description: The README calls out arithmetic, comparisons, logical operators, and the MKS equality spelling ?=.

sections.title: break & continue
sections.description: Loop control is part of the language tour: use break to leave a loop and continue to skip to the next pass.

sections.title: Strings & output
sections.description: Strings are first-class values in examples and are commonly printed through Writeln.
points: Strings use double quotes.
Tests cover string output, s[1] indexing, upper(), lower(), and len().
Writeln accepts multiple arguments, so you can print text and values together.
For function-style wrappers, see std/string.

sections.title: std/array workflow
sections.description: Use std/array when you want named helper functions around array methods.
points: push(arr, v) calls arr.inject(v) and returns the same array.
pop(arr) removes and returns the last item through the stdlib helper.
first(arr) and last(arr) return null when the array is empty.
map, filter, and reduce expect a function argument, so keep those examples small and tested.

tabs.label: MKS
tabs.note: This is based on std/array.mks. The repo tests directly cover the underlying methods inject, len, offset, exclude, and purge.

sections.title: Loop recipes: where to put state
sections.description: Most MKS loop bugs come from placing state in the wrong spot. Keep setup before the loop, updates inside or in the for step, and exits close to the condition that needs them.
points: For while, state is usually prepared before the loop: counter, accumulator, current index.
State updates must be guaranteed. If there is a continue;, update the counter before it or use for.
for is useful when init/condition/step fit clearly in one header.
repeat is best for a simple “do this N times”; repeat i in N is for when you need the pass number.

tabs.label: MKS
tabs.note: The while example mirrors tests/cases/loop_control_01.mks. The update happens before continue;, so the loop can still finish.

sections.title: Comments
sections.description: Single-line comments use //. The repo docs and tests use them for notes beside code.
code: var x =: 10; // note beside a statement

sections.title: Numbers
sections.description: Numbers are core values. Arithmetic test cases cover integer-looking literals with + - * / %.

sections.title: null
sections.description: null is listed as a value in the reference. The documented array last helper returns it for empty arrays.

sections.title: std/bool
sections.description: Boolean helpers are bundled in std/bool. Tests cover true, false, not, and, and or.

sections.title: Comparison operators
sections.description: Comparison tests cover equality, inequality, greater/less, and inclusive bounds.

sections.title: Expression grouping
sections.description: Nested expression tests show that normal precedence and parentheses are handled.

sections.title: Empty arrays
sections.description: Empty arrays can be created, measured, then mutated with inject.

sections.title: Nested arrays
sections.description: Nested array literals and chained indexing are covered by tests.

sections.title: Array mutation methods
sections.description: The tests exercise several mutating array methods. Keep their exact behavior tied to runtime tests while the language is experimental.

sections.title: Write and Writeln
sections.description: The reference documents Write and Writeln. Examples mostly use Writeln for readable test output.

sections.title: Early return from if
sections.description: A function can return from inside an if block. Put the fallback return after the block.

sections.title: Recursive factorial
sections.description: The recursion test uses a classic base case plus recursive multiplication.

sections.title: More std/math names
sections.description: The stdlib exposes more math helpers than the short README example shows.
sections.warning: Examples here list available names. For edge-case numeric behavior, verify against the current MKS version.

sections.title: String method examples
sections.description: String examples cover empty, len, upper, and lower, plus wrappers in std/string.

sections.title: std/string wrappers
sections.description: std/string exposes function-style wrappers over string and array methods.

sections.title: std/time
sections.description: std/time wraps runtime time helpers.
sections.warning: Verify time formatting and exact helper behavior against the current MKS version; public documentation is still thin here.

sections.title: Duplicate imports
sections.description: The README says modules run once. A test imports the same file twice and notes that the duplicate should be skipped.
python: import math_module  # duplicate import uses module cache

sections.title: Module file example
sections.description: The repository root includes a small math.mks used by import tests.

sections.title: Updating entity fields
sections.description: Entity methods can update fields through self. The entity test boosts a stored numeric field.

sections.title: Defer order
sections.description: Current runtime code stores deferred blocks and executes them from newest to oldest when leaving the block.

sections.title: Watch timing
sections.description: Register the watcher and handler before the assignment that should trigger it.

sections.title: Errors
sections.description: Readable errors with source positions and hints.
